q: Seebeck effect refers to conversion of:
exp: Seebeck effect produces emf due to temperature difference between two junctions.

q: In a thermocouple, emf is produced when:
opts: Both junctions are at same temperature
One junction is heated and other is cold
exp: Temperature difference between junctions generates thermoelectric emf.

q: Peltier effect is associated with:
opts: Heating/cooling at junction due to current
exp: Peltier effect causes heat absorption or evolution at junction when current flows.

q: Thomson effect occurs in:
exp: Thomson effect is heat absorption/emission along a single conductor with temperature gradient and current.

q: The thermoelectric emf depends mainly on:
exp: EMF in Seebeck effect depends on temperature difference between junctions.

q: A thermocouple is most commonly used for measuring:
exp: Thermocouples are widely used for temperature measurement in high-temperature ranges.

q: The Seebeck coefficient has unit:
exp: Seebeck coefficient is emf per unit temperature difference.

q: If both junctions of a thermocouple are at same temperature, emf is:
exp: No temperature difference means no thermoelectric emf.

q: Peltier effect is the inverse of:
exp: Peltier and Seebeck effects are thermodynamic reversals of each other.

q: In Seebeck effect, emf increases when:
exp: Thermoelectric emf is proportional to temperature difference.

q: Which device works on Seebeck effect?
exp: Thermocouples convert temperature difference into voltage.

q: In Peltier effect, heat absorbed or released is proportional to:
exp: Peltier heat ∝ current flowing through junction.

q: Which material pair is commonly used in thermocouples?
exp: Dissimilar metals like copper-iron are commonly used.

q: Thomson effect is observed when:
opts: Current flows in uniform temperature conductor
Current flows in temperature gradient conductor
exp: Requires both current and temperature gradient.

q: Direction of thermoelectric current depends on:
exp: Depends on Seebeck coefficient and temperature gradient.

q: Which law relates heat absorbed in Peltier effect?
exp: Peltier heat is proportional to current.

q: Thermoelectric emf is maximum when:
opts: Both junctions are at same temperature
exp: Greater temperature difference produces higher emf.

q: Seebeck effect is based on:
opts: Charge diffusion due to temperature gradient
exp: Charge carriers diffuse due to temperature difference.

q: Which effect is responsible for thermoelectric generator?
exp: Seebeck effect converts heat directly into electricity.

q: In thermocouple, emf produced is typically:
exp: Thermocouple voltages are usually in millivolt range.

q: If cold junction temperature increases, thermoelectric emf:
exp: Temperature difference decreases, so emf reduces.

q: Peltier effect occurs at:
exp: Occurs at junction of dissimilar conductors.

q: Thermoelectric series is used to:
exp: Series helps determine polarity of emf.

q: In Seebeck effect, electrons flow from:
opts: Hot to cold or vice versa depending on materials
exp: Direction depends on Seebeck coefficients of materials.

q: Thermoelectric power is another name for:
exp: Thermoelectric power refers to Seebeck coefficient.

q: Which effect involves both heating and cooling depending on current direction?
exp: Peltier effect reverses with current direction.

q: In thermoelectric effects, energy conversion is mainly between:
exp: Thermoelectric devices convert heat to electricity or vice versa.

q: Seebeck effect is widely used in:
exp: Thermocouples are practical Seebeck devices.

q: Which is correct about thermoelectric emf?
opts: Depends on materials and temperature difference
exp: Both material pair and temperature difference matter.

q: Thomson effect is usually:
exp: Thomson effect is generally small compared to others.

q: Which law is NOT part of thermoelectric effects?
exp: Newton’s law is unrelated to thermoelectricity.

q: Thermocouple emf is zero when:
opts: Both junctions are at same temperature
exp: No temperature gradient means no emf.

q: Which is true for thermoelectric effects?
exp: They involve coupling of thermal and electrical energy.

q: Seebeck emf increases with:
exp: EMF is proportional to temperature difference.

q: Which device is based on Peltier effect?
exp: Peltier devices are used for cooling applications.

q: Direction of thermoelectric emf depends on:
exp: Both factors determine polarity.

q: Which statement is correct?
exp: All three effects are thermodynamically related.

q: In thermocouple, maximum emf is achieved by:
exp: Larger temperature difference increases emf.

q: Thermoelectric effects obey:
exp: They are governed by thermodynamics.

q: Which junction is heated in Peltier effect depends on:
exp: Reversing current reverses heating/cooling.

q: Thermocouple output voltage is generally:
exp: Output is small DC voltage proportional to temperature difference.

q: Seebeck effect is primarily used for:
exp: Thermocouples measure temperature differences.

q: Which of the following is a thermoelectric effect?
exp: Seebeck is one of the three thermoelectric effects.
